title: Rolling Bar Cart with Removable Tray
summary: A 1x3 bar cart on wood-round wheels, with a top tray that lifts off for serving and a bottom tray that stays fixed for storage.
description:
The wheels are the standout detail: solid wood rounds bolted through the legs rather than purchased casters, which keeps the whole cart looking like furniture instead of an appliance on wheels. Getting them right means drilling a true center hole in each round and test-fitting with the bolts before final assembly — confirm you're happy with rolling clearance and looks before trimming the legs to their final length, since that step is hard to undo.

The two trays are sized deliberately differently: the top tray is built 1/4" narrower than the bottom one specifically so it lifts off cleanly without scratching the finish on the frame as it comes free. That small dimensional difference is doing real work, not an arbitrary tolerance — keep it when you cut, even though it seems like it shouldn't matter at that scale.

tools.note: Cuts the handle profile into the side aprons — clamp both together and sand until they match.

cutList.note: Draw the handle pattern first, clamp both together, and sand until they match. Handle overhangs the back leg by up to 6".
1/4" narrower than the bottom tray so it lifts free without rubbing.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two side aprons at 41-1/2", four legs at 32", two end aprons at 16", two side apron supports (optional) at 32", four tray sides (both trays) at 33-1/4", ten bottom tray slats at 14-1/2", ten top tray slats at 14-1/4", two bottom tray ends at 16" and two top tray ends at 15-3/4" from the Pine. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to nine 8 ft Pine. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the two sides
steps.body:
Draw a handle pattern on the side aprons — the handle can be up to 6" long, since it overhangs the back leg by that amount. Clamp both side aprons together and sand until they match perfectly. Optionally, taper the back leg with a jigsaw for a lighter look.

Attach the legs to the side aprons with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws (jig set for 3/4" stock) and glue, keeping the handle overhang consistent and adjusting for square. Build two identical sides, keeping the pocket holes on the inside faces to conceal them.

steps.title: Join the sides with the end aprons
steps.body: Once both sides are complete, attach the end aprons between them with glue, adjusting for square as you go.

steps.title: Add the optional side supports
steps.body: Adding a side apron support gives extra rigidity and a rail that a smaller tray or drop-in ice bucket could rest on later. This step is optional but recommended.

steps.title: Build the two trays
steps.body: Pocket holes are overkill here — use 2" finish nails and wood glue instead, two nails per slat, with slats spaced 3/4" apart. Remember the top tray is built 1/4" narrower than the bottom one, so it lifts free without scratching the finish as it comes off.

steps.title: Install the trays
steps.body: The top tray simply rests in place on the frame. For the bottom tray, measure up 6" from the base and screw it in place with 1-1/4" screws and glue from the insides, two per leg.

steps.title: Add the wheels
steps.body:
Drill a center hole in each wood round with a 1/2" bit. Mark and drill matching holes in the legs. Test-fit the wheels with the bolts, and once you're satisfied with the fit and rolling clearance, remove them and trim the legs to final length if needed.

For the permanent installation, use three washers per wheel: one on the bolt side, one on the nut side, and one between the wheel and the leg to protect the finish. Bolt heads face out, nuts on the inside.

steps.title: Sand, finish, and add handles
steps.body: Sand every surface, finish, and attach the handles.

images.alt: Rolling bar cart with a removable top tray
